coredata.dc:title: A paper-based visualization chip based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots nanoprobe for Hg(Ⅱ) detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Chengyue
Liu, Zhenping
Wang, Xianfeng
Liu, Huan
Yang, Mei
Huo, Danqun
Hou, Changjun
coredata.dc:description: Hg(II) is one of the most toxic heavy metal ions. The bioconcentration and degradation-resistant of Hg(II) bring about serious harm to the ecosystem and humans. Therefore, the establishment of an accurate and effective method for detecting mercury ions is of great significance to environmental protection, food safety and human health. In this work, a new fluorescent nanoprobe was presented using nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) for Hg(II) sensing with high stability and selectivity. On this basis, a paper-based chip was innovatively developed for visualization detection of Hg(II). The N-CQDs were prepared through a one-step hydrothermal reaction using catechol and ethylenediamine as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. As-prepared N-CQDs exhibit the strong green fluorescence at the excitation/emission wavelength of 370/511 nm. In aqueous solution, a rapid and highly sensitive detection method of Hg(II) was established by the joint of dynamic and static quenching effect of Hg(II) on N-CQDs fluorescence. Under the optimized conditions, there was a stable correlation between the fluorescence intensity change of N-CQDs and the concentrations of Hg(II) in the range of 15 ∼ 104 nM, and the detection limit was down to 8 nM (S/N = 3). The recoveries of water, sorghum and rice were 91.60 to 102.46%, which was consistent with ICP-MS. More importantly, the N-CQDs nanoprobe was further integrated in nitrocellulose membrane to develop paper-based chip for Hg(II) visualization detection, and the detection performance was also excellent. This strategy had significant implications for achieving low-cost, on-site real-time monitoring of mercury (II) in the environment and food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mercury ions
Fluorescence sensing detection
Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
Paper-based visualization chip

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Hg(II) using N-CQDS nanometer probe
The detection mechanism of Hg(II) by N-CQDs
Highly sensitive detection of Hg(II) in real samples
Fabrication and application of paper-based visual chip

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Project of China ( 2017YFC1602000 ), Chongqing science and technology commission ( CSTC2018jcyjAX0062 ), Brew Microorganisms Technology and Application of Key Laboratory Project in Sichuan Province (No. NJ2018-01 ), Graduate Scientific Research and Innovation Foundation of Chongqing, China ( CYB20070 ), Chongqing Higher Education Teaching Reform Research Project ( 203249 ), Chongqing Graduate Tutor Team Construction Projectandand, Analytical and Testing Center of Chongqing University for TEM / the characterization of FT-IR / XPS /XRD and the sharing fund of Chongqing University's large equipment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Hydro-thermal synthesis of N-CQDs and Hg(II) induced fluorescence quenching of N-CQDS, (B) Construction of paper-based sensing chips loaded with N-CQDs and visual detection of Hg(II).

HRTEM image (A) and size distribution (B) of N-CQDs, (C) Optical property of N-CQDs (Inset: under UV light(a) and visible light(b)), (D) Feasibility of N-CQDs nanoprobe for Hg(II) sensing. (Inset: before (a) and after (b) the addition of Hg(II)).
Full scan XPS spectrum of N-CQDs (A), high-resolution spectra of C1s (B), N1s (C), O1s (D).
XRD image (A) and the FTIR spectra of the N-CQDs before and after the addition of Hg(II) (B).
The fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs before and after addition of Hg(II) (A), the absorption spectrums of Hg(II), N-CQDs and their mixture (B).
Influence of pH (A) and response time (B) for Hg(II) detection, the excitation-dependent fluorescence properties of N-CQDs (C).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs after the addition of different concentration of Hg(II), (B) Fluorescence response of N-CQDs with the increasing concentration of Hg(II). Inset: linear fitting diagram between fluorescence response and the concentration of the Hg(II). The error bar represents the mean standard error of the three measurements.
The selectivity evaluated for N-CQDs nanoprobe of Hg(II) detection (the mix sample represented the interference of other metal ions in the presence Hg(II)).
pH stability (A) and Fluorescence stability (B) of N-CQDs.

(A) Response of N-CQDs paper based chips to different concentrations of Hg(II) ; (B) Digital image analysis was performed using ImageJ to determine the average intensity of N-CQDs and the Hg(II) concentration calibration curve is plotted.
The fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs solution before and after Hg(II) addition.
Comparison of N-CQDs probe with other methods for detection of Hg(II).

head.ce:title: A paper-based visualization chip based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots nanoprobe for Hg(Ⅱ) detection

ce:note-para.ce:italic: Those authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The N-CQDs were prepared through a one-step hydrothermal reaction using catechol and ethylenediamine as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The rapid and highly sensitive detection method of Hg(II) was established by the joint of dynamic and static quenching effect of Hg(II) on N-CQDs fluorescence (A), and the N-CQDs nanoprobe was further integrated in nitrocellulose membrane to develop paper-based chip for Hg(II) visualization detection, and the detection performance was also excellent (B).

Hg(II) is one of the most toxic heavy metal ions. The bioconcentration and degradation-resistant of Hg(II) bring about serious harm to the ecosystem and humans. Therefore, the establishment of an accurate and effective method for detecting mercury ions is of great significance to environmental protection, food safety and human health. In this work, a new fluorescent nanoprobe was presented using nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) for Hg(II) sensing with high stability and selectivity. On this basis, a paper-based chip was innovatively developed for visualization detection of Hg(II). The N-CQDs were prepared through a one-step hydrothermal reaction using catechol and ethylenediamine as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. As-prepared N-CQDs exhibit the strong green fluorescence at the excitation/emission wavelength of 370/511 nm. In aqueous solution, a rapid and highly sensitive detection method of Hg(II) was established by the joint of dynamic and static quenching effect of Hg(II) on N-CQDs fluorescence. Under the optimized conditions, there was a stable correlation between the fluorescence intensity change of N-CQDs and the concentrations of Hg(II) in the range of 15 ∼ 104 nM, and the detection limit was down to 8 nM (S/N = 3). The recoveries of water, sorghum and rice were 91.60 to 102.46%, which was consistent with ICP-MS. More importantly, the N-CQDs nanoprobe was further integrated in nitrocellulose membrane to develop paper-based chip for Hg(II) visualization detection, and the detection performance was also excellent. This strategy had significant implications for achieving low-cost, on-site real-time monitoring of mercury (II) in the environment and food.

ce:section.ce:para: Hg(II) is a highly toxic metal ion, and widely exists in a variety of media, such as industrial wastewater, soil, crops, etc. Hg(II) can be easily assimilated in human tissues and accumulated in human organs through biological amplification of food chain, threatening human life and health. Life is endangered in severe cases [1]. At present, there were many methods for detection of Hg(II), such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [2], atomic emission spectrometry (AES) [3], atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS) [4] and Mass spectrometry (MS) [5]. However, these methods usually rely on large instruments and professional operators, which costs a lot of financial, material resources and time, so it was difficult to meet the needs of on-site and real-time detection. In view of the importance of monitoring Hg(II), it was necessary to develop a new method to identify Hg(II) quickly and easily.

In recent years, the electrochemical sensors, colorimetric sensors and fluorescence sensors based on nanomaterials had attracted wide attention due to their advantages of easy operation, fast response speed and high sensitivity [6–8]. However, compared to fluorescence sensing, electrochemistry involves in reference electrodes and complex electrode modification which requires frequent maintenance and calibration, the sensitivity of colorimetric sensors is low and often can not meet the detection requirements [9]. Although it can be used for continuous on-line monitoring, it also suffers from the interference from conductivity and other kinds of analytes. In contrast, fluorescence sensing was easier to realize rapid detection. In addition, due to its portability, low cost, strong operability, and the ability to realize the visual detection of analytes without additional means, paper-based sensors were widely used in the field real-time detection, which has become a research hotspot in recent years. In today's emphasis on environmental protection and food safety, combining the advantages of both fluorescence analysis method and paper-based sensor, it is of great significance for the realization of on-site, sensitive, specific, visual and rapid quantitative detection of targets.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a kind of carbon-based nanomaterials with particle size less than 10 nm, which have attracted much attention since they were first discovered in 2004 [10]. CQDs had been widely used in sensors, light-emitting diodes, biological imaging and other fields due to its excellent properties such as good water solubility, high chemical inertia, stable photoluminescence, photobleaching resistance and low toxicity. In this study, based on the nanomaterial nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), fluorescence nanoprobe and paper-based visual chip were constructed to realize rapid detection of Hg(II). The fluorescent N-CQDs were synthesized by hydro-thermal carbonisation (HTC) in one step using cost-effective compounds catechol and ethylenediamine to break the restriction of high cost of precursor materials in the previous CDs synthesis. The process was simple and environmentally friendly. The N-CQDs had the advantages of low toxicity. The synthetic aromatic hydrocarbon polymers had good fluorescence properties. In addition, N-doped CQDs can further improve the detection performance and specific selectivity [11,12].

The green fluorescence N-doped CQDs synthesized in this paper had a maximum emission wavelength of 511 nm at 370 nm excitation, and the N-CQDs exhibited excellent optical properties. Based on the joint of dynamic and static quenching, a “off” fluorescence nanoprobe for Hg(II) detection was constructed. In this sensing system, Hg(II) can quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs, so as to realize the rapid and specific recognition of Hg(II). Based on the above mechanism, a paper-based fluorescence sensor for the visual detection of mercury ions was innovatively prepared in this study. Under the optimized conditions, the paper-based fluorescence nanoprobe had good stability and excellent selectivity for the detection of Hg(II), which indicated that this study had a great application prospect and development potential for the field detection of Hg(II).

Catechol and Ethylenediamine (EDA) were purchased from Chuandong Chemical Reagent Factory. Tris-HCl was purchased from Solibol Technology Co., Ltd.. CaCl2, Hg(NO3)2, MgCl2, NaCl, CuSO4, CoCl2, Ba(NO3)2, CdCl2, AgNO3, CrCl3, KCr2O7 and NiCl2 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd.. MnCl2, Pb(NO3)2, FeCl3 and AlCl3 were purchased from Beijing Chemical Industry group CO., Ltd.. Dialysis bag (MD31:500D) was obtained from Shanghai Titan Technology Co. Ltd. The water used in the experiments was deionized water obtained from Molecular Water System CO., Ltd..

TEM (ZEISS LIBRA 200,Carl Zeiss AG), FTIR (IRAffinity-21,Shimadzu Corporation of Japan), XPS (GG314-JPS-9200, Westernization Instrument Technology Co., Ltd), XRD(Bruker AXS D5005,Bruker AXS, Germany) were used to characterize the morphology and particle size, surface functional groups, element distribution and formation, and crystal structure of the materials, respectively. Luminescence spectrophotometer (LS-55, PerkinElmer, USA), UV–visible spectrophotometer (TU-1901,Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd, China) were used to record the optical properties of different processes respectively. Deionized water used in the experiment was prepared by an ultra-pure water machine (Milli-Q Academic A10, Milliple Corporation, USA). FA2004 Electronic Balance (Hengping Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) was used to weigh experimental reagents. Solid samples were obtained by freeze-drying process (FD-1A-50, Wuxi Jiuping Instruments Co., Ltd., China). An ultraviolet transmission analyzer (UV-3C, Zhuhai Black Horse Medical Instrument Co., Ltd., China) was used to observe fluorescence properties in the naked eye. The fluorescence lifetime of the CQDs was recorded with the FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Zeta potential were measured by Zeta Sizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK).

Fluorescent N-CQDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method using catechol and ethylenediamine as precursors. The specific operations were as follows: 275.3 mg catechol and 0.15 mL ethylenediamine were added in 25 mL distilled water and treated by ultrasonication for 10 min to form a uniform mixture. The above mixture was transferred to a 50 mL PTFE-lined steel autoclave and heated at 180℃ for 10 h. After that, the precipitate was removed by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min. A dialysis bag (MW31:500) was used to remove the unreacted molecules for purification of the N-CQDs for 24 h. The preparation of N-CQDs and the detection of Hg(II) was shown in Fig. 1 .

In order to solve the inconvenience of large-scale instrument for field detection, we prepared N-CQDs paper-based chips (Fig. 1B). Qualitative nitocellulose filter membrane was cut into squares with side length of 10 mm and soaked in ethanol solution for 5 min to remove surface impurities, and then washed with deionized water for three times. After that, the filter membrane was immersed in N-CQDs solution for 30 min, and then taken out and dried in oven at 37 ℃. The N-CQDs-based membrane was pasted on the black plastic sheet, and titrated with 5 μL Hg(II) solution with different concentrations. The fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs-based membrane was directly radiated under UV lamp and the digital image of fluorescence change was obtained by a smartphone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg(II) using N-CQDS nanometer probe
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the sensitivity of the N-CQDs nanoprobe for the detection of Hg(II), the synthesized N-CQDs was appropriately diluted with Tris-HCl buffer (0.01 M, pH = 6.5). 100 μL of Hg(II) with different concentrations were added to 400 μL N- N-CQDs diluents, then a certain volume of Tris-HCl buffer (0.01 M, pH = 6.5) was added to make the final volume of detection system up 1 mL. The mixture was fully shaken and stood still for 3 min, then the fluorescence intensity of the reaction system with Hg(II) different concentrations was measured at the emission wavelength of 511 nm after the excitation of 370 nm. All above experiments were carried out at room temperature.

In order to evaluate the practicability of N-CQDs nanoprobe for the detection of Hg(II), the lake water (Minzhu Lake in District A of Chongqing University), sorghum and rice purchased from Wal-Mart Supermarket in Shapingba District of Chongqing were selected as the actual samples for the detection of Hg(II) using N-CQDs nanoprobe. The water sample was filtered by 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove the water-insoluble particulate impurities. The pretreatment of the sorghum and rice samples were performed by microwave digestion to extract Hg(II). 0.3 g rice (or sorghum) powder and 8 mL nitric acid were mixed in a digestion tank, then covered and soaked overnight. The lid of the tank was tightened to digest the sample powder according to the microwave digestion conditions in the literature [13]. After digestion and cooling, the lid was opened slowly for exhausting. The digestive trough was placed on a temperature controlled electric heating plate and heated at 80 ℃ for 1 h to remove brown gas. The digestive juices were transfer to a 50 mL volumetric flask, then the sodium hydroxide solution and hydrochloric acid were used to adjust the solution pH to 6.5. After mixing, the volume was measured and then proceed detection or stored at 4 ℃ for later use.

HRTEM was used to characterize the morphology of N-CQDs. Fig. 2 A showed that the synthesized N-CQDs had uniform size with spherical shape and good dispersion. The N-CQDs possessed the crystalline structure with an interlayer lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (inset in Fig. 2A), and this was consistent with previous reports [14]. As can be seen from Fig. 2B, N-CQDs exhibited a relatively narrow size distribution ranging from 1.00 nm to 3.40 nm. The average diameter of N-CQDs was about 2.40 nm by statistical analysis of 100 random nanoparticles. The optical properties of N-CQDs were explored. As shown in Fig. 2C, the optimal excitation wavelength of N-CQDs was 370 nm, and the emission wavelength was 511 nm. N-CQDs showed the green fluorescence under ultraviolet light (inset a) and brown solution under visible light (inset b). In addition, as shown in Fig. 2D, N-CQDs showed the obvious fluorescence quenching after the addition of Hg(II) indicating that the probe was feasible for Hg(II) detection.

The elemental composition of N- CQDs was characterized by XPS. It can be seen from the energy spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. 3 A) that three peaks appeared at 286 eV, 399 eV and 531 eV, which was correspond to the characteristic peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. It indicated that N-CQDs was composed of C, N and O elements [15]. The high-resolution energy spectrum of C1s (Fig. 3B) showed three main peaks, whose binding energies were 284.0 eV for the C-C bond, 285.2 eV for the C-N bond, and 287.3 eV for the C-O bond. The N1s high-resolution energy spectrum (Fig. 3C) showed that the binding energies were 398.7 eV, 399.8 eV and 401.8 eV, respectively, indicating the existence of N-H bond, C-N = C bond, and N-C bond. It can be inferred from O1s high resolution energy spectrum (Fig. 3D) that O-H bond, C-O bond and C = O bond have the corresponding binding energy at 530.9 eV, 532.2 eV and 533.3 eV, respectively. The above test results indicated the successful synthesis of N-CQDs.

Fig. 4 B showed the FTIR spectrum results to identify the functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs. The strong bands at 3000–3450 cm−1 were caused by the O-H and N-H groups [16,17], while the absorption bands at 1550–1650 cm−1 were corresponding to the C-O/C = O bond [18]. The peak bands at 1230 cm−1 and 1340 cm−1 were derived from C-N vibrations [19]. The results of FTIR were consistent with those of XPS. Nitrogen-doping to the carbon structure/framework and owing rich hydrophilic groups on N-CQDs surface were confirmed by XPS and FTIR spectroscopy techniques. This was the advantage of catecol and ethylenediamine as precursors for synthesizing nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots [20,21]. In the X-ray diffraction characterization of N-CQDs, it was found that there was an obvious wide peak at 20.19° (Fig. 4A), indicating that the structure was similar to the crystal face of graphene (002) [22,23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection mechanism of Hg(II) by N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Hg(II) reacted easily with the complex, causing ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) effect, leading to fluorescence quenching. The high sensitive and selective fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs caused by Hg(II) may be attributed to the following two mechanisms. Firstly, compared with other metal ions, Hg(II) had a larger radius and a stronger affinity for nitrogen, making it easier to form stable non-fluorescent complexes with N-CQDs [24]. Secondly, fluorescence was quenched due to N-CQDs aggregation. Hg(II) may combine with multiple nitrogen and oxygen atoms of N-CQDs simultaneously, and this complexation process may lead to the formation of aggregates then the electronic structure of N-CQDs changed, the final result was the non-radiative electron/hole composite annihilation [25].

It was well known that fluorescence quenching caused by the reaction between fluorescence molecules and quenchers included static quenching and dynamic quenching [26], or both of these two processes [27]. In this work, when Hg(II) was added into N-CQDs aqueous solution, the fluorescence of N-CQDs was quenched due to the interaction between Hg(II) and N-CQDs. It can be seen from Fig. 5 A that the fluorescence lifetime attenuation curve changed after adding Hg(II) into N-CQDs solution, which preliminarily indicated that the fluorescence quenching by Hg(II) belonged to dynamic quenching [28]. In order to more accurately prove the mechanism of this quenching, the mean fluorescence lifetimes of N-CQDs solution before and after adding Hg(II) were analyzed, and the test results were fitted by a double exponential function. As shown in Table 1 , the average fluorescence life of N-CQDs aqueous solution was 7.27 ns, and the average fluorescence life of N-CQDs was 7.05 ns after the complete fluorescence quenching with the addition of large concentration of Hg(II). Before and after the addition of Hg(II), the fluorescence life changed significantly. As shown in Fig. 5B, the ultraviolet absorption spectra of N-CQDs/Hg(II) system was different from that of individual N-CQDs or Hg(II), indicating that a stable complex was formed between Hg(II) and N-CQDs. The main mechanism was that Hg(II) coordinates with –COOH and –NH2 on the surface of N-CQDs to form the complex [29], and this can be seen from the FTIR spectra before and after the addition of Hg(II) (Fig. 4B). The amino peak at 3000–3450 cm−1 and the carboxyl absorption peak at 1550~1650 cm−1 of N-CQDs were significantly weakened after response with Hg(II). The Zetapotential analysis was used to characterize the surface charge of N-CQDs, and their results indicated the occurrence of combination betweenN-CQDs and Hg(II). The results showed that the Zeta potentials were 8.9 mV and 11.5 mV before and after the addition of Hg(II) in N-CQDs solution. The significantly increased Zeta potential after response with Hg(II) confirmed that the effect of N-CQDs and Hg(II) was the ligand-to-metal binding charge transfer [30,31]. The results of ultraviolet absorption spectra and Zeta were the characteristics of static quenching [28,32]. The plot of F0/F (or t0/t) versus the concentrations of the quencher (Q) during a single static quenching or dynamic quenching should be a straight line according to the Stern-Volmer (SV) equation [27]. It can be seen from Fig. 7B that the SV diagram was not a straight line but a curve, suggesting a joint of dynamic and static quenching for the detection mechanism of Hg(II) by N-CQDs.

In order to improve the detection performances of the fluorescent probe, the pH value and reaction time of the sensing system were optimized. Firstly, the fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs nanoprobe before and after adding Hg(II) were recorded under different pH (3.0–7.0) to study the effect of pH on the detection of Hg(II). As shown in Fig. 6
A, the difference in fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs-Hg (II) system reached the maximum when pH was 6.5, which resulted from that the carboxyl groups and other functional groups on N-CQDs surface can be easily protonated under low pH conditions. In contrast, Hg(II) tended to hydrolyzed at higher pH. In both cases, chelation between Hg(II) and N-CQDs would be reduced. Thus, pH of 6.5 was selected as optimal pH of Hg(II) detection. Secondly, we optimized the response time between Hg(II) and N-CQDs. As can be seen from Fig. 6B, the fluorescence signal of the detection system was basically stable after reaction for 3 min. 3 min was selected as the response time for detecting Hg(II). In addition, the excitation-dependent fluorescence properties of N-CQDs were investigated to determine the optimal excitation wavelength. As seen in Fig. 6C, under different excitation wavelengths, all the emission wavelengths of N-CQDs appeared at 511 nm, and the fluorescence emission was strongest at 370 nm excitation wavelength. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs at 511 nm was not dependent on the excitation wavelength. In this probe, 370 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength for the detection of fluorescence signal.

In order to explore the selectivity of N-CQDs for Hg(II) detection, the interference of other 16 common metal ions on the fluorescence signal of the nanoprobe was performed. The results were shown in Fig. 8 , the mix sample represented the interference of other metal ions in the presence Hg(II). Hg(II) played an obvious fluorescence quenching effect to the nanoprobe system. On this basis, the change of fluorescence signal after adding interference ion could be ignored. Compared with other metal ions, the excellent selectivity of N-CQDs nanoprobe for Hg(II) may be attributed to the stronger interaction between Hg(II) and N-CQDs [33,34]. These results indicated that N-CQDs nanoprobe can selectively detect Hg(II).

In order to obtain a more stable nanoprobe, the fluorescence stability of N-CQDs was evaluated. Stability of N-CQDs nanoprobe under different pH conditions was shown in Fig. 9 A. The N-CQDs nanoprobe exhibited a weak fluorescence signal at low pH. With the increase of pH, the fluorescence signal first increased and then declined. It reached the maximum at pH of 6.5, which was consistent with the optimal pH for detection of Hg(II). The results showed that N-CQDs had good pH stability at pH of 6.5 [35–38]. In addition, the fluorescence stability of N-CQDs was investigated by long-term monitoring the fluorescent intensity. As shown in Fig. 9B, slight fluorescence decrease within 16 weeks indicated the fluorescence performance of N-CQDs had a good stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Highly sensitive detection of Hg(II) in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of N-CQDs nanoprobe system for the determination of Hg(II) was evaluated using three actual samples, including the lake water of Minzhu Lake in District A of Chongqing University, sorghum and rice from the local Wal-Mart supermarket. These three samples induced the ignorable fluorescence decline of nanoprobe system, indicating the concentration of Hg(II) in these real samples is lower than LOD of the N-CQDs nanoprobe system. When different concentrations of Hg(II) solutions were added to these actual samples, the recoveries were between 91.60 and 102.46%, and the relative standard deviations (n = 3) were between 2.38 and 6.04%, which were consistent with the results of ICP-MS analysis (Table 3 ). The consistency between the detection value and the expected value indicated that the N-CQDs nanoprobe can achieve the accurate detection of Hg(II) in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and application of paper-based visual chip
ce:section.ce:para: N-CQDs paper-based visualization chip was prepared according to the method in part 2.3. Hg(II) solutions with different concentrations were titrated on the chips. Under the irradiation of UV lamp at 375 nm wavelength, fluorescence images of the paper-based chips were taken using a smart phone. As shown in Fig. 10 A, quenching effect of Hg(II) at different concentrations (0 ∼ 75 μM) on paper-based chip was recorded. The calibration curves were developed by studying the relationship between the mean fluorescence intensity values of N-CQDs and different concentrations of Hg(II) (recorded from the ImageJ software analysis). As shown in Fig. 10B, a reasonable linear relationship between the average fluorescence intensity and Hg(II) concentration was obtained in the range of 1 ∼ 50 μM (visible to the naked eye). The fitting equation was obtained as Y = -0.14X + 254.18 R2 = 0.9972, and the limit of detection was 0.8 μM. In order to verify the practicability of N-CQDs paper-based visualization chip, the recoveries rate in real samples (lake water, sorghum and rice) were tested using proposed assay after simple treatment. In each sample, the scalar of Hg(II) was 1, 2.5 and 5 μM, respectively. The recoveries were in the range of 91.8 ∼ 102.5%, and RSDs were lower than 4.36% (n = 3).

The above results indicated that the N-CQDs paper-based visualization chip developed in this study can selectively identify Hg(II) in a variety of different samples. The application of smartphone simplified the analysis device to realize rapid visual detection of Hg(II) in the field. This not only provided a simple and effective method for monitoring Hg(II) contamination in the environment and food, but also provided a new idea for the development of new detection methods. Therefore it had great potential value in future practical applications.

In this paper, a new method of Hg(II) detection based on N-CQDs fluorescent nanoprobe was developed and successfully integrated into a paper-based chip for the first time for visual monitoring of Hg(II). The device was easy to construct, and N-CQDs paper-based chip was successfully prepared by loading the fluorescent nanoprobe onto the surface of nitrocellulose membrane using a simple physical adsorption method. Based on the joint of dynamic and static quenching of Hg(II) on the fluorescence of N-CQDs nanoprobe, the visual detection of Hg(II) can be easily performed by the change of quenching area (visible to the naked eye) of the paper-based chip. The LOD of this method was calculated to be 0.8 μM using ImageJ software for digital imaging and analysis of paper-based chips. The liquid N-CQDs nanoprobe is specifically selective for Hg(II) and has a wide detection range (15 nM~10 μM) with an LOD as low as 8 nM. The construction of solid-state paper-based chip solved the limitation that large instrument was inconvenient to monitor Hg(II) in situ. Finally, the labeled recovery experiments were carried out in the real samples of environmental water, sorghum and rice, which proved that the method had the potential of practical application.

Chengyue Zou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Zhenping Liu: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Xianfeng Wang: Writing– original draft. Huan Liu: Visualization, Investigation. Mei Yang: Software, Validation. Danqun Huo: Supervision. Changjun Hou: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Project of China(2017YFC1602000), Chongqing science and technology commission(CSTC2018jcyjAX0062), Brew Microorganisms Technology and Application of Key Laboratory Project in Sichuan Province(No. NJ2018-01), Graduate Scientific Research and Innovation Foundation of Chongqing, China (CYB20070), Chongqing Higher Education Teaching Reform Research Project(203249), Chongqing Graduate Tutor Team Construction Projectandand, Analytical and Testing Center of Chongqing University for TEM / the characterization of FT-IR / XPS /XRD and the sharing fund of Chongqing University's large equipment.
